title: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
preview: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, ...
text: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.

title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
preview: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language f...
text: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language for enterprise vs non-enterprise (and how to handle “Waiting on Customer” without gaming the SLA). The group agreed to standardize a short “page template” (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys, customer tier, and what changed), require linking the SUP Jira ticket in the bridge, and to use a consistent cadence phrased as targets rather than guarantees. Follow-ups include edits to the Confluence SLA table wording, Jira automation for required fields + SLA timers, and incident-bot improvements to pin SUP ticket links in bridge channels.

title: Zenlytics x Redwood — Dedicated overload mitigation + remediation SLA alignment
preview: Zenlytics asked how Redwood handles overload on Dedicated (429s, queueing, model/region fallback) and how remediation SLAs will be communicated and tracked. Redwood walked through current Dedicated behavior (admission control, rate limiting vs capacity saturation, graceful degradation options), and the Reliability P...
text: Zenlytics asked how Redwood handles overload on Dedicated (429s, queueing, model/region fallback) and how remediation SLAs will be communicated and tracked. Redwood walked through current Dedicated behavior (admission control, rate limiting vs capacity saturation, graceful degradation options), and the Reliability Program changes: incident taxonomy classification, owner mapping, and new remediation SLA timers (time-to-mitigate/time-to-fix) keyed by severity and tier. We aligned on what counts as an SLA clock start, carve-outs (customer-caused traffic spikes, cloud provider/regional outages), and the comms path (Dedicated email + optional shared Slack channel, post-incident review, dashboard visibility).

title: Reliability update: regional failover automation + Dedicated protections
preview: Redwood shared an update on the Reliability Program work focused on regional failover automation (signal hierarchy, guardrails/rollback, observability) and how Dedicated customers are protected during regional impairment and during planned controlled failover tests (“game days”). Atlas asked about blast radius, chan...
text: Redwood shared an update on the Reliability Program work focused on regional failover automation (signal hierarchy, guardrails/rollback, observability) and how Dedicated customers are protected during regional impairment and during planned controlled failover tests (“game days”). Atlas asked about blast radius, change approvals, data residency constraints, audit evidence, and how/when Redwood communicates during planned tests vs real incidents. Redwood confirmed Dedicated traffic is isolated, failover automation is gated (per-tenant limits + optional approval hooks), and planned tests will have explicit opt-in/notice plus tight scopes. Next steps include sharing the comms playbook, audit event schema/retention guidance, and aligning a customer-specific “do not fail over across residency boundary” policy.

title: Proxima Bank - escalation check-in (capacity protection / 429s)
preview: Follow-up after Proxima’s Jan escalation tied to burst traffic on /v1/chat/completions. Redwood reviewed the new hot-route capacity protection behavior (tier-aware admission control + consistent 429 reason codes). Proxima confirmed stability improved (lower tail latency, fewer 5xx) but noted increased 429s during mo...
text: Follow-up after Proxima’s Jan escalation tied to burst traffic on /v1/chat/completions. Redwood reviewed the new hot-route capacity protection behavior (tier-aware admission control + consistent 429 reason codes). Proxima confirmed stability improved (lower tail latency, fewer 5xx) but noted increased 429s during morning batch + interactive overlap. Teams aligned on which 429s are expected vs actionable, and on an exception process for temporary burst increases (with guardrails and advance notice).

title: Controlled Failover Game Day Briefing (prod) - us-east to eu-west
preview: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and wh...
text: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and who has authority to approve execution/rollback.

Topics (a...

title: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
preview: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + determ...
text: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, observability, runbooks, game days) and out-of-scope items (multi-cloud migration, serving runtime rewrite, SLA language). Open questions focused on trigger hierarchy (authoritative signals vs corroboration), anti-flap behavior, approval model (auto vs ack-required), and how to safely run controlled production failovers with limited blast radius.

title: Acme AI - Technical deep dive: priority routing, SLOs, and overload behavior
preview: Acme AI asked for a deep dive on Redwood’s new hot-route capacity protection (priority routing + tier-aware quotas) for /v1/chat/completions and embeddings. Team aligned on enterprise SLO targets (p95/p99 latency + availability), what happens under overload (admission control, backpressure, controlled shedding), and...
text: Acme AI asked for a deep dive on Redwood’s new hot-route capacity protection (priority routing + tier-aware quotas) for /v1/chat/completions and embeddings. Team aligned on enterprise SLO targets (p95/p99 latency + availability), what happens under overload (admission control, backpressure, controlled shedding), and how 429s are classified (quota vs overload) with retry guidance. Acme’s main concerns were noisy-neighbor incidents, predictability during bursts, and auditability of throttling decisions. Redwood proposed a 2–3 week pilot in us-east with allowlisted enterprise keys, dashboards, and weekly SLO review; discussed optional Dedicated as a follow-on if they need stricter isolation.

title: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
preview: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quot...
text: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.

title: Aurora Health Systems x Redwood — technical deep dive: overload behavior + SLAs
preview: Aurora asked detailed questions about Redwood’s new hosted overload protections: what errors look like (429 vs 503), whether Retry-After is always present, how to avoid retry storms, and how noisy-neighbor isolation works across tenants and tiers. Redwood described circuit breaker layering (gateway admission + routi...
text: Aurora asked detailed questions about Redwood’s new hosted overload protections: what errors look like (429 vs 503), whether Retry-After is always present, how to avoid retry storms, and how noisy-neighbor isolation works across tenants and tiers. Redwood described circuit breaker layering (gateway admission + routing + runtime backpressure), tenant/tier-aware policies, stable overload error codes with correlation IDs, and optional graceful degradation (fallback model/region, feature reduction like max_tokens caps / streaming concurrency). Aurora emphasized healthcare reliability expectations and asked how overload-induced failures map to SLAs/SLO reporting. Redwood committed to share the overload error taxonomy doc + sample responses, confirm Retry-After behavior across routes, and propose a pilot plan and SLA language for Hosted vs Dedicated.

title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
preview: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature ...
text: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.

title: Dedicated Beta - Staged Rollouts check-in (gates + Console feedback)
preview: Beta check-in with Lattice AI on Dedicated staged rollouts. Customer likes the stage timeline concept and promotion audit trail, but reported friction around (1) gate strictness (p99 latency + 429/drop thresholds) and noisy failures during low-traffic periods, (2) confusion about what "saturation/headroom" means per...
text: Beta check-in with Lattice AI on Dedicated staged rollouts. Customer likes the stage timeline concept and promotion audit trail, but reported friction around (1) gate strictness (p99 latency + 429/drop thresholds) and noisy failures during low-traffic periods, (2) confusion about what "saturation/headroom" means per stage, and (3) Console UX gaps: unclear gate evidence, limited per-region breakdown, and too many clicks to promote/hold/rollback. Redwood aligned on tuning: minimum sample size, better missing-metrics messaging, and UI improvements (gate evidence panel, annotations, and simplified promote confirmation).

title: Weekly POC check-in — experiment results + guardrails (Northbridge)
preview: Northbridge walked through their first 48 hours of route-level experiment traffic. They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft...
text: Northbridge walked through their first 48 hours of route-level experiment traffic. They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft-stop defaults, guardrail evaluation windows, known propagation lag and attribution drop issues) and agreed on changes: clearer UI copy/tooltips for deltas, making the evaluation window explicit, and tightening guidance around stickiness keys and streaming retry attribution. Next steps include Redwood validating attribution integrity for their route, suggesting tuned thresholds for p99 latency and error rate, and a short enablement note on how to interpret variant headers and results freshness.

title: Dedicated upgrade safety – staged rollouts / gates / rollback semantics (technical deep dive)
preview: Lattice AI asked for a detailed walkthrough of how Redwood will do safer Dedicated pool upgrades using staged rollouts. Redwood described the rollout plan model (stages, gates, promote/abort/hold) and emphasized deterministic, auditable actions with automatic abort/rollback on gate failure. The group focused on how ...
text: Lattice AI asked for a detailed walkthrough of how Redwood will do safer Dedicated pool upgrades using staged rollouts. Redwood described the rollout plan model (stages, gates, promote/abort/hold) and emphasized deterministic, auditable actions with automatic abort/rollback on gate failure. The group focused on how stages map to reserved capacity (traffic splitting vs replica partitions), avoiding starving small pools during canary, and how to handle missing/noisy metrics. Lattice AI emphasized strict change management (audit logs, RBAC, reason codes), and asked about rollback semantics for streaming and multi-region behavior if one region degrades mid-stage.
